era_context: The Classical Greek world during the Persian Wars. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I is at its height, having invaded Greece with a massive army and navy. The Greek city-states, led by Athens and Sparta, are mounting coordinated resistance. This year marks the pivotal military and naval campaigns that will determine whether Greece remains independent or falls under Persian rule. Simultaneously, the Indian subcontinent sees the rise of early republics and kingdoms; China is in the Spring and Autumn Period under the Zhou Dynasty.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
Mesoamerica (no contemporary written records; archaeological evidence only)

events.title: Battle of Thermopylae
events.description: A Greek hoplite force of approximately 7,000 men, including 300 Spartans under King Leonidas I, held the narrow Thermopylae pass against a vastly larger Persian army for three days in late summer. The Greeks were eventually outflanked via a mountain path and forced to retreat, but the delaying action allowed Athens to evacuate and gave the Greek alliance time to organize. The battle became a symbol of Greek resistance and sacrifice, though it was ultimately a Persian tactical victory.
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple Greek sources written within 50–400 years of the event. The basic facts (location, participants, outcome) are secure, though casualty figures and tactical details vary between sources.

events.title: Battle of Salamis
events.description: The Athenian navy, under the command of Themistocles, decisively defeated the Persian fleet in the narrow Strait of Salamis in September. The Greek trireme fleet of approximately 370 ships destroyed or captured roughly 200 Persian vessels. The victory was decisive because it prevented Persian amphibious operations and forced Xerxes to withdraw the bulk of his army back to Asia Minor, effectively ending the invasion. The battle demonstrated the superiority of Greek naval tactics and the vulnerability of the Persian fleet in confined waters.

sources.name: Aeschylus, Persae (The Persians) — a contemporary Greek tragedy

events.certainty_note: Aeschylus' play was performed in 472 BCE, only 8 years after the battle, and contains eyewitness-informed details. Herodotus provides a detailed narrative. The basic outcome is secure; tactical details and casualty figures show variation.

events.title: Evacuation of Athens
events.description: Following the Persian advance after Thermopylae, the Athenian population—estimated at 200,000–300,000 including slaves—was evacuated from the city. The civilian population was transported to the island of Salamis and the Peloponnese, while the city itself was occupied and burned by Persian forces. This mass evacuation was unprecedented in Greek history and required extraordinary logistical coordination. The evacuation preserved the Athenian population and allowed the city to be rebuilt after the Persian withdrawal.
events.certainty_note: The evacuation is well-attested in Greek sources. The scale and timing are secure; exact population figures are estimates based on later sources.

events.title: Confucius' death (traditional date)
events.description: According to traditional Chinese chronology, Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551–479 BCE) died in the spring of 479 BCE, though some sources place his death in 480 BCE. Confucius was a philosopher and teacher whose ideas about ethics, governance, and social relationships would profoundly shape Chinese civilization and East Asian thought for over two millennia. His teachings, preserved in the Analects (Lunyu), emphasize ritual propriety (li), filial piety, and virtuous governance. His death marked the end of his direct teaching but the beginning of the Confucian tradition's expansion.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian, compiled c. 109 BCE

events.certainty_note: The traditional date of Confucius' death is 479 BCE (some sources say 480 BCE). No contemporary records exist; the date comes from later compilations. Modern scholars debate whether the exact year can be verified, but the general period (late 6th to early 5th century BCE) is secure.

events.title: Reign of Xerxes I in Persia
events.description: Xerxes I (r. 486–465 BCE) was the Achaemenid king who launched the invasion of Greece. In 480 BCE, he was at the height of his power, commanding the largest military force assembled in the ancient world up to that time. The invasion of Greece was intended to punish the Greek city-states for their support of the Ionian Revolt and to expand Persian dominion into Europe. Though the invasion ultimately failed, Xerxes maintained control of the Persian Empire and continued to rule for another 15 years.
events.certainty_note: Xerxes' reign dates and the invasion are well-attested in both Greek and Persian sources. The chronology is secure.

events.title: Rise of the Magadha Empire in India
events.description: During the 5th century BCE, the Magadha kingdom in the Gangetic plain was emerging as a major power in northern India. By 480 BCE, Magadha was consolidating control over neighboring territories and establishing itself as a dominant force. The kingdom would eventually become the seat of the Mauryan Empire in the 4th century BCE. This period also saw the rise of early republics and oligarchies (mahajanapadas) across northern India, including the Licchavis and Vrijjis, which are attested in Buddhist and Jain texts.
key_figures: Bimbisara (King of Magadha, r. c. 543–491 BCE)

sources.name: Pali Canon (Buddhist texts), particularly the Jataka tales
Arthashastra (attributed to Kautilya, compiled later but referencing earlier traditions)

events.certainty_note: The rise of Magadha is attested in Buddhist and Jain texts, but these were compiled centuries after the events. The chronology of early Indian kingdoms is debated among scholars; dates are approximate within ±10–20 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Thermopylae emphasizes Greek heroism and sacrifice, but modern scholarship notes that the battle was a tactical Persian victory and that the Greek forces were not as outnumbered as Herodotus claimed (he reports Persian forces of over 1 million, which is almost certainly exaggerated). The strategic significance of Salamis is sometimes overstated in popular accounts; while decisive, it did not immediately end the war—fighting continued into 479 BCE. Regarding Confucius' death, the exact year (479 vs. 480 BCE) is disputed, and no contemporary records confirm the date; it rests entirely on later compilations. The 'rise of Magadha' is inferred from later Buddhist and Jain texts with no contemporary epigraphic confirmation for 480 BCE specifically.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 480 BCE is heavily weighted toward Greek sources, particularly Herodotus, who wrote 50+ years after the events and whose account, while invaluable, contains legendary elements and Persian casualty figures that are almost certainly inflated. Aeschylus' Persae provides a near-contemporary Greek perspective but is a dramatic work, not a historical chronicle. Persian sources are sparse; Achaemenid inscriptions exist but do not detail the Greek campaign in depth. Indian sources (Buddhist and Jain texts) are much later compilations and reflect religious rather than purely historical interests. Chinese sources for Confucius are similarly late. The year is well-documented for the Mediterranean world but poorly documented for most of Asia and Africa.

graph_edges.note: The Greek retreat from Thermopylae enabled the concentration of Greek naval forces at Salamis, which led directly to the naval victory.
The Persian naval defeat at Salamis forced Xerxes to withdraw and led to the final land battle at Plataea in 479 BCE.
The Persian breakthrough at Thermopylae necessitated the evacuation of Athens.
Confucius' death marks the end of his direct teaching but the beginning of the Confucian school's expansion and systematization.
